text.title: Usability Testing Rules and Severity Rating
text.summary: Concrete rules for task wording, participant behavior during sessions, severity classification, and test types.

rule.statement: Tasks must be scenario-based, not instruction-based: describe a situation and goal, never name UI elements or describe navigation steps.
rule.rationale: Instruction-based tasks ("click the account button") test whether users can follow directions, not whether the design is discoverable. Scenario tasks ("you need to update your address") test the actual usability of the design.

rule.statement: Each task must have a defined success criterion before the session begins.
rule.rationale: Without pre-defined success criteria, observers disagree on whether the user succeeded, making severity rating unreliable and findings non-comparable across sessions.

rule.statement: Never help participants during tasks, even when they are clearly stuck and frustrated.
rule.rationale: Every moment of struggle is data showing where the design fails. Helping invalidates both that observation and the participant's remaining task data.

rule.statement: Separate observation (what happened) from inference (why it happened) in session notes.
rule.rationale: Observers who record inferences contaminate data. Write "user clicked Help three times before finding Settings" not "user was confused by the navigation." Agents can infer later from clean observation data.

rule.statement: Rate every finding on a four-level severity scale before prioritizing recommendations.
rule.rationale: Without severity rating, teams treat all findings equally, spending effort on minor friction while critical blockers remain unfixed.

list.item: Critical: user cannot complete the task — fix before any release
High: significant difficulty completing the task — fix before launch
Medium: some struggle but task completed — fix soon after launch
Low: minor friction, task completed easily — fix when possible

rule.statement: Use participant count and task-blocking status together to assign severity: blocked AND 60%+ of participants = Critical; blocked OR 60%+ = High; 40-60% = Medium; below 40% = Low.
rule.rationale: Frequency data prevents over-weighting verbally expressive participants whose observations sound severe but are idiosyncratic.

list.item: Moderated: facilitator present — use for complex tasks that need probing and follow-up questions
Unmoderated: remote automated — use for scale, quick validation, and metric collection
Think-aloud: user verbalizes thoughts — use to understand mental models and decision-making
First-click: where users click first — use to test navigation and information architecture
